edges.description: Impaired GABAergic signaling disrupts reciprocal inhibition in motor circuits, causing simultaneous contraction of agonist and antagonist muscles.

nodes.description: Anti-GAD65 antibodies target glutamic acid decarboxylase, the enzyme that synthesizes GABA from glutamate. This leads to reduced GABA production and impaired inhibitory neurotransmission, resulting in neuronal hyperexcitability. GAD65 is predominantly localized to synaptic vesicles and provides additional GABA synthesis when there is increased demand for rapid neurotransmitter release during high-frequency neural firing.

evidence.reference_title: Autoantibodies to glutamic acid decarboxylase in a patient with stiff-man syndrome, epilepsy, and type I diabetes mellitus.
evidence.snippet: A band comigrating with glutamic acid decarboxylase in sodium dodecyl sulfate-polyacrylamide gels appeared to be the only nervous-tissue antigen recognized by cerebrospinal fluid antibodies

evidence.reference_title: Stiff-person Syndrome and GAD Antibody-spectrum Disorders: GABAergic Neuronal Excitability, Immunopathogenesis and Update on Antibody Therapies.
evidence.snippet: Very high serum anti-GAD antibody titers are a key diagnostic feature for all GAD-SD, commonly associated with the presence of GAD antibodies in the CSF, a reduced CSF GABA level

evidence.reference_title: Therapies in Stiff-Person Syndrome: Advances and Future Prospects Based on Disease Pathophysiology.
evidence.snippet: caused by impaired GABAergic inhibitory neurotransmission and autoimmunity characterized by very high titers of GAD antibodies and increased GAD-IgG intrathecal synthesis

nodes.description: Failure of GABAergic interneurons to inhibit antagonist motor neurons during voluntary movement, resulting in co-contraction of opposing muscle groups and the characteristic rigid posture.

evidence.reference_title: Stiff-person Syndrome and GAD Antibody-spectrum Disorders: GABAergic Neuronal Excitability, Immunopathogenesis and Update on Antibody Therapies.
evidence.snippet: the phenomenon of reciprocal inhibition as the fundamental mechanism of the patients' muscle stiffness and spasms
